Wild plums at various points along the Kay's Creek Parkway.  Best trees are past the stream on the north side of the trail.  There yellow, orange, and red varieties.  The plums are small, but you can gorge yourself with as many as you can handle. Access: Publi

Not sure if they're still here, but on the south side of the pond, along the public walking path, there are some blackberries. Access: Public. Falling Fruit season: July-August.
This tree is located on WSU property but is very accessible from the public street.  Makes a good syrup. Access: Public. Falling Fruit season: September-November.

This tree is on Weber State Campus. If you come after campus is closed or on a weekend you shouldn't have any issues accessing this. If you do come during business hours I would pick discretely. Access: Public. Falling Fruit season: August-September.
A green apple tree. It usually has a plentiful fruiting, but there are worms in the apples of course. Access: Public.
Grapes that overhang a fence and various other fruit trees. Access: Private but overhanging.

Two plum trees, one completely dead, the other half dead, next to a bench and table in between the pavilion and the bathrooms. Bring a step ladder and maybe a blanket to lay down as you shake the ripe plums loose. Access: Public.
This tree is located in someones backyard.  It hangs over the fence to be quite visible from the street. Access: Private. Falling Fruit season: August-October.

Some kind of wild plum tree with small fruit that's yellow with reddish blush when ripe. It's down in a sort of gulley between the river trail and the stream. Hard to forage from, as it overhangs the stream. The fruit often has worms. Delicious if you want to
